idea.hook: Can you see the Big Bang's first light?
facts: For ~380,000 years after the Big Bang, the universe was a dense, opaque plasma.
As it expanded and cooled to ~3,000 Kelvin, electrons formed atoms, making space transparent.
This released a flood of light – the 'Cosmic Microwave Background' (CMB) – which we still detect today.
idea.payoff: This ancient light, now cooled to just 2.7 Kelvin, is the oldest thing we can ever observe – the universe's baby picture!
idea.title: See The Universe's BABY PICTURE! 📸🌌

scenes.text: Can you see the Big Bang's first light?
scenes.visual: A swirling vortex of light and energy, implying the beginning of the universe.

scenes.text: For ~380k years, universe was opaque plasma.
scenes.visual: An animated depiction of a dense, fiery red/orange plasma sphere, particles colliding rapidly.

scenes.text: Cooled to 3,000K, atoms formed, space cleared.
scenes.visual: The plasma sphere cools, changing to yellow/white. Electrons visibly combine with nuclei to form atoms, and streaks of light burst outwards.
The light expands, transforming into faint, uniform microwave radiation ripples filling space. A subtle 'CMB' label appears over the ripples.

scenes.text: CMB: Universe's baby picture, oldest light at 2.7K!
scenes.visual: A stunning, detailed full-sphere map of the Cosmic Microwave Background (e.g., Planck or WMAP data), slightly pulsing with color variations, rotating slowly.
